papers.title: Runaway Young Stars in the Orion Nebula

abstract.text: The star forming region of the Orion Nebula (ONC) is ideal to study the stellar dynamics of young stars in a clustered environment. Using Gaia DR2 we search for the pre-main sequence stars with unusually high proper motions that may be representative of a dynamical ejection from unstable young triple systems or other close three-body encounters. We identify twenty-six candidate stars that are likely to have had such an encounter in the last 1 Myr. Nine of these stars could be traced back to the densest central-most region of the ONC, the Trapezium, while five others have likely interactions with other OB-type stars in the cluster. Seven stars originate from other nearby populations within the Orion Complex that coincidentally scattered towards the ONC. A definitive point of origin cannot be identified for the remaining sources. These observations shed light on the frequency of the ejection events in young clusters.

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper presents a novel, high-precision survey of proper motions in the Orion Nebula Cluster using Gaia data, enabling the identification of previously undetected runaway stars and providing a robust statistical characterization of dynamical instabilities in young star clusters. Evaluation of candidates: Nine high proper motion stars, including Brun 259 and V1961 Ori, are traced back to the Trapezium region with similar trajectories and ejection times (~0.1 Myr ago), suggesting recent dynamical interactions in this dense, massive core of the ONC. Discussion and Conclusions: This study identifies 26 stars in the Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC) that were ejected via 3-body dynamical interactions within the last 1–2 Myr, with a disk fraction of 32±13% among ejected stars—slightly higher than the cluster average—demonstrating that such extreme interactions are not rare and likely contribute significantly to the cluster's dynamical evolution, despite limitations in detection due to velocity dispersion and observational biases. Selection of candidate ejected stars: The selection of candidate ejected stars from the Orion A cluster involves filtering a large catalog of young stars using photometric and proper motion criteria to remove older field stars and low-mass main sequence contaminants, ultimately identifying 1867 likely young, runaway stars.

papers.title: A hyper-runaway white dwarf in Gaia DR2 as a Type Iax supernova primary remnant candidate
papers.author_names: Nicholas J. Ruffini Andrew R. Casey

abstract.text: Observations of stellar remnants linked to Type Ia and Type Iax supernovae are necessary to fully understand their progenitors. Multiple progenitor scenarios predict a population of kicked donor remnants and partially-burnt primary remnants, both moving with relatively high velocity. But only a handful of examples consistent with these two predicted populations have been observed. Here we report the likely first known example of an unbound white dwarf that is consistent with being the fully-cooled primary remnant to a Type Iax supernova. The candidate, LP 93-21, is travelling with a galactocentric velocity of v$_{gal}$ $\\\\simeq$ 605 km s$^{-1}$, and is gravitationally unbound to the Milky Way. We rule out an extragalactic origin. The Type Iax supernova ejection scenario is consistent with its peculiar unbound trajectory, given anomalous elemental abundances are detected in its photosphere via spectroscopic follow-up. This discovery reflects recent models that suggest stellar ejections likely occur often. Unfortunately the intrinsic faintness of white dwarfs, and the uncertainty associated with their direct progenitor systems, makes it difficult to detect and confirm such donors.

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: LP 93-21’s velocity of over 400 km s⁻¹, inconsistent with dynamical ejections from globular clusters or the Galactic center and exceeding expected speeds from core-collapse supernovae, strongly supports a binary SN Ia/Iax ejection scenario, with the SD (sub-dwarf) scenario most plausible, where it was a He-burning donor star that accreted mass and was ejected at high speed after a supernova explosion. Conclusions: LP 93-21 is the closest hyper-runaway white dwarf candidate to the Sun, providing strong evidence for a SN Iax-ejection scenario and potentially representing a key missing piece in the understanding of high-velocity, cooled-down WD remnants from SNe Ia/Iax progenitors. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework for identifying donor remnants (DRs) and sub-luminous SN Iax ejecta as observational tracers of SN Ia progenitor systems, solving the long-standing problem of distinguishing between single-degenerate and double-degenerate scenarios by linking SN Ia explosions to observable signatures in surviving companion stars. Observations: LP 93-21 is a hyper-runaway white dwarf with a high galactic velocity and unbound orbit, making it the closest known hyper-runaway star to the Sun.

title: A hyper-runaway white dwarf in Gaia DR2 as a Type Iax supernova primary remnant candidate

catalog_assessment.evidence: Focuses on single hyper-runaway white dwarf LP 93-21 with detailed observational data including galactocentric velocity, spectroscopic follow-up, and abundance analysis
